COVID-19.title: Evolution of mRNA Technology(COVID-19)
COVID-19.content: The development of mRNA technology has accumulated over several decades: from the discovery of mRNA in the 1960s to scientists gradually overcoming key obstacles such as its instability, high immunogenicity, and delivery challenges. Pieter Cullis and others developed ionizable cationic lipids that allow mRNA to be safely encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs), while Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman reduced mRNA's immunogenicity and enhanced protein expression by incorporating pseudouridine, establishing a foundation for mRNA as a therapeutic modality. Building on these breakthroughs, RNA therapeutics and LNP technology first achieved clinical success in siRNA treatments such as patisiran, providing validation and platform capabilities for the rapid development of subsequent mRNA vaccines. When the COVID-19 pandemic emerged in 2020, the flexibility and rapid design potential of mRNA became evident, enabling vaccines to progress from sequence information to clinical application within months. Today, mRNA technology has expanded into various vaccines, gene therapies, and potential personalized medicine applications, and its broad applicability and rapid manufacturing characteristics position it as a leading frontier in nucleic-acid-based therapeutics.

COVID-19.title: Real-World Effectiveness of COVID-19 Vaccines
COVID-19.content: After completing the full course of COVID-19 va  ccination, the risks of infection, hospitalization, severe illness, and death are all substantially reduced. In real-world settings, vaccines not only greatly decrease the likelihood of becoming infected but also effectively prevent disease progression when infection does occur, allowing the vast majority of vaccinated individuals to avoid hospitalization or life-threatening outcomes. The degree of protection varies across different populations: healthcare workers generally experience the highest level of protection, likely due to younger age and better overall health; protection is slightly lower among the general adult population; and older adults—whose immune systems tend to respond less robustly—show somewhat reduced vaccine effectiveness. Differences are also observed among vaccine types, with mRNA vaccines providing stronger protection against infection, while inactivated vaccines show more moderate effects. Overall, regardless of vaccine type, vaccination markedly lowers the risk of severe disease and death following infection and remains one of the most effective strategies for reducing the burden of COVID-19.

COVID-19.title: Definition and Impact of Long COVID
COVID-19.content: Long COVID generally refers to chronic health conditions that persist for months after infection with the virus that causes COVID-19. Although there is currently no universal definition, it is widely viewed as an infection-associated chronic illness. Patients may experience more than 200 symptoms documented in research, including extreme fatigue, memory or cognitive difficulties, dizziness, changes in taste or smell, as well as issues related to respiration, cardiovascular function, sleep, and digestion. This condition can affect anyone who has been infected with COVID-19, even if their acute symptoms were mild or unnoticed. Some individuals may also develop other illnesses, such as heart disease, mood disorders, or autonomic dysfunction. Because symptoms can last a long time and significantly affect daily life, medical evaluation and ongoing care are essential, while research continues to advance our understanding of the mechanisms of Long COVID and to improve treatment and support strategies.

COVID-19.title: Effects of Early Pandemic Lockdowns(COVID-19)
COVID-19.content: Early COVID-19 lockdowns—implemented worldwide amid profound uncertainty about the virus's transmission, severity, and the absence of effective medical interventions—played a meaningful role in slowing early disease spread and reducing deaths, though at substantial economic, social, and psychological cost. Research indicates that the effectiveness of these measures depended heavily on timing, local conditions, and complementary non-pharmaceutical interventions; while some studies found strong health benefits, others highlighted the influence of demographic and structural factors or noted that individuals had already begun to change behavior voluntarily. Economic analyses increasingly suggest that although lockdowns caused sharp short-term contractions, successful early containment sometimes enabled quicker rebounds. As vaccines later became available, their high effectiveness in reducing infections diminished the relative value of lockdowns. Overall, evidence suggests that early, time-limited lockdowns can be a useful emergency tool in the initial stages of a dangerous pandemic, but their costs and variable effectiveness underscore the importance of preparedness, targeted interventions, and rapid deployment of medical countermeasures.

COVID-19.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Global Healthcare Systems
COVID-19.content: The healthcare systems of many countries experienced widespread and sustained disruption during the COVID-19 pandemic, with essential medical services significantly affected. Substantial declines were observed across key public health programs—including cancer screening, tuberculosis detection, and HIV testing—with reductions in some countries ranging from 26% to 96%. Maternal healthcare, family planning, hospital deliveries, and the management of chronic diseases were also notably impacted, resulting in large numbers of missed visits per 1,000 births or per 1,000 population in certain settings. Childhood vaccination rates fell by more than 10% in multiple countries, leading to hundreds of thousands of missed doses of critical vaccines. Reduced healthcare utilization stemmed from fear of infection, economic hardship, the diversion of health workers and facilities to COVID-19 care, and mobility barriers created by lockdown policies. Significant differences in system resilience were evident across countries, influenced by factors such as workforce organization, hospital bed capacity, and the strength of existing public health infrastructures. Overall, these findings highlight the profound impact of the pandemic on global healthcare systems and underscore the need to strengthen health system resilience to maintain essential services during future crises.

COVID-19.title: Global Vaccine Distribution Inequality(COVID-19)
COVID-19.content: The study indicates that although countries have committed, through global health treaties, to share vaccines and other public health resources equitably, wealthy nations in practice often prioritize stockpiling vaccines for their own populations. Based on a non–peer-reviewed modeling analysis, the research team compared two scenarios: one in which about 50 high-income countries monopolize the first 2 billion doses of COVID-19 vaccines, and another in which vaccines are allocated according to each country's population size. The model assumes a single-dose vaccine and examines two versions with 80% and 65% efficacy respectively, estimating the number of deaths that could be prevented under a total of 3 billion doses administered. The results show that under population-based equitable distribution, an 80%-efficacy vaccine could reduce global deaths by 61%, whereas under a stockpiling strategy the reduction would be only 33%; for a 65%-efficacy vaccine, the figures are 57% and 30% respectively. The study further notes that the marginal gains high-income countries receive from monopolizing vaccines are far smaller than the substantial losses faced by low-income countries. For instance, under the 80%-efficacy scenario, Western Europe could avert 74% of deaths with a non-cooperative strategy compared to 55% with equitable distribution, whereas Western Africa would avert only 13% versus as high as 93%, and South-Eastern Asia 5% versus 62%. Overall, the modeling results suggest that although real-world distribution strategies may not be strictly binary, equitable global access to vaccines can minimize mortality across all countries and represents the most favorable approach promoted by mechanisms such as COVAX.

Russia-Ukraine.title: Russia's 2022 Full-Scale Invasion of Ukraine
Russia-Ukraine.content: On 24 February 2022, Russia launched a full-scale land, sea, and air invasion of Ukraine, aiming to rapidly seize the country and overthrow its government, but it faced strong resistance from Ukrainian forces and civilians and failed to capture key cities including the capital, Kyiv. The invasion caused large numbers of civilian casualties and massive refugee flows, and triggered widespread global condemnation; most UN member states demanded the withdrawal of Russian troops, and Western countries imposed unprecedented sanctions on Russia while providing Ukraine with substantial military and financial assistance. After suffering setbacks in the early phase of the invasion, Russian forces shifted their focus to offensive operations in eastern and southern Ukraine and continued large-scale missile and drone attacks that severely damaged Ukraine's energy systems and urban infrastructure. During the second half of 2022 and throughout 2023, Ukraine launched multiple counteroffensives and regained parts of its territory, while Russia held referendums in occupied areas—rejected by the international community—and implemented mobilization measures. As the war became protracted, the frontlines settled into a stalemate by late 2023 to 2024, but Russian forces achieved new advances with the capture of Avdiivka in February 2024, while Ukraine continued its resistance bolstered by ongoing Western support, including a major U.S. aid package passed in 2024. Throughout the conflict, multiple countries and international organizations accused Russian forces of committing war crimes in Ukraine and initiated related investigations.

Russia-Ukraine.title: Europe's Refugee Response to the War(Russia-Ukraine)
Russia-Ukraine.content: After the outbreak of the Russia-Ukraine war, Europe quickly adopted a systematic set of measures to respond to the large-scale displacement of Ukrainians. These included activating and later extending the Temporary Protection Mechanism to provide immediate and collective access to legal residence, employment, education, and social services for Ukrainian nationals and certain non-Ukrainian residents who had been living in Ukraine before 24 February 2022 and were forced to flee because of the war. The EU also delivered humanitarian assistance, civil protection support, technical assistance, and border-management aid to internally displaced people in Ukraine and to major host countries such as Poland, Czechia, Slovakia, and the Republic of Moldova. In addition, the EU reallocated cohesion policy funds, increased pre-financing, and mobilized multiple funding instruments to help member states meet needs related to housing, healthcare, education, and childcare. The EU further promoted affordable roaming arrangements and established a common framework for transitioning out of temporary protection, with the aim of supporting the sustainable return and reintegration of Ukrainians when conditions allow, or enabling those who remain in the EU to shift to other lawful residence statuses. Together, these measures constitute a coordinated and long-term European response that balances refugee protection with the capacities of member states.

Russia-Ukraine.title: Ukrainian Defense and the Battle for Kyiv
Russia-Ukraine.content: Russia's initial invasion plan in 2022 aimed to conduct a rapid strike from Belarus, advance toward Kyiv through the Chornobyl corridor, and seize the Hostomel airfield to airlift reinforcements, thereby encircling and capturing the capital within hours. However, disorganized units, poor intelligence and communication, insufficient air and missile preparation, and a severe misjudgment of Ukraine's willingness to resist caused the plan to falter early. With intelligence support from Western partners, Ukraine deployed limited but critical defensive forces; non-combat National Guard personnel and subsequently arriving artillery units blocked Russian reinforcements at Hostomel and halted the advance along the Bucha–Irpin line. The large Russian convoy moving toward Kyiv suffered continuous losses due to supply problems and exposure to artillery fire and ambushes, while the Ukrainian leadership's decision to remain in the capital helped stabilize the defense. After roughly 35 days of fighting, mounting losses and stalled progress forced Russian forces to withdraw from the Kyiv axis, preventing the fall of the capital.

Russia-Ukraine.title: International Military and Economic Support for Ukraine
Russia-Ukraine.content: Nearly two years after the outbreak of the Russia–Ukraine war, the American public still broadly supports providing economic assistance to Ukraine—for example, U.S. financial support and economic aid programs—with 58% of Americans favoring continued economic assistance. In terms of military support, a majority of Americans (also 58%) support supplying Ukraine with additional weapons and military equipment, including further deliveries of arms and related materiel. These levels of support have declined from the early stages of the war in 2022, when 79% backed military aid, with particularly notable decreases among Republicans and Independents, while Democratic support has remained consistently high. In addition, public support for Ukraine’s accession to NATO remains strong, but support for sending U.S. troops to fight in Ukraine is very low. At the same time, most Americans believe that the security relationship with Ukraine helps strengthen U.S. national security.

Health.title: Global Health Burden of Tobacco Use
Health.content: The global use of tobacco imposes a substantial health burden, primarily due to the release of numerous toxic and carcinogenic chemicals during smoking. These substances induce oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, endothelial dysfunction, and genotoxicity, which in turn lead to cardiovascular diseases, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, at least 17 types of cancer, and damage across multiple organ systems. In 2019, tobacco use contributed to approximately 7.7 million deaths and 200 million disability-adjusted life years. Long-term smokers face a mortality rate about three times that of never-smokers and lose at least 10 years of life expectancy on average. Tobacco-related harms extend beyond smokers themselves, as environmental tobacco smoke (ETS) exposes non-smokers—particularly children—to significant risks such as lung cancer, coronary heart disease, and sudden infant death syndrome. Although tobacco use often begins during adolescence and is strongly addictive, smoking cessation can substantially reduce disease risks, with health benefits increasing over time. Comprehensive public health measures—such as preventing youth smoking, providing cessation counseling, and offering pharmacological treatments—are essential to reducing the global health burden caused by tobacco.

Health.title: Antimicrobial Resistance as a Major Threat to Global Public Health
Health.content: In the field of global public health, antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is rapidly evolving into a profound and complex systemic threat. Its primary drivers stem from the persistent overuse and misuse of antimicrobial agents in human medicine, animal husbandry, and agricultural production, which subject pathogens to prolonged selective pressure and enable them to acquire resistance through mechanisms such as enhanced efflux pump activity, biofilm formation, receptor downregulation, and genetic mutation. Meanwhile, antibiotic residues in animal waste, agricultural soil, and the food chain facilitate the circulation and exchange of resistance genes in the environment, further accelerating the spread of AMR. Resistant infections have already resulted in substantial health and economic burdens, including millions of AMR-associated deaths worldwide and economic losses reaching hundreds of billions of dollars, with low- and middle-income countries—where surveillance capacity and resources are often limited—being disproportionately affected. In addition, rising temperatures and ecological pressures linked to climate change may enable certain pathogens to evolve heat-tolerant or drug-resistant traits, thereby intensifying the worsening AMR trend. Confronted with this cross-species, cross-sector global challenge, international organizations—particularly the World Health Organization (WHO)—have identified AMR as a critical public health threat and incorporated it into the One Health framework, integrating medical, agricultural, and environmental surveillance and multi-sector interventions to strengthen antimicrobial stewardship, enhance data-monitoring capacity, raise public awareness, and support research on alternative therapies, with the aim of curbing the spread of resistance and informing future control strategies.

Health.title: Importance of HPV Immunization
Health.content: The HPV vaccine plays a crucial role in public health by preventing multiple cancers and genital warts associated with human papillomavirus. It helps the body build immune responses to specific HPV strains, reducing the risk that an infection will persist long term and progress to cancer. Health authorities recommend routine vaccination at ages 11–12 and completing catch-up doses before age 26 to ensure maximum protection. Even individuals who have already become sexually active or have been infected with certain HPV types may still benefit from protection against strains they have not yet encountered. Research indicates that the vaccine has a strong safety profile, with most side effects limited to mild reactions at the injection site. Although vaccination significantly lowers the risk of disease, it does not replace cervical cancer screening, so ongoing screening according to guidelines remains essential.

Health.title: Rising Obesity and Its Societal Consequences
Health.content: More than one billion people worldwide are living with obesity, and over the past three decades, obesity prevalence has more than doubled among adults and quadrupled among adolescents aged 5–19. In most regions, obesity has already surpassed underweight as the dominant form of global malnutrition. Obesity is associated with more than 200 comorbidities, including cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and multiple types of cancer, and is linked to increased mortality risk, nutrient deficiencies, and declines in cognitive and physical functioning. The rise in obesity is driven by factors such as economic development, the industrialization of food systems, increased consumption of ultra-processed foods, and reductions in physical activity due to more sedentary lifestyles, while rising food costs and climate change have further exacerbated nutritional imbalances. The societal consequences of obesity manifest in several ways: health risks are unevenly distributed across socioeconomic groups; low-income populations face structural barriers to accessing healthy foods, participating in physical activity, and maintaining a healthy lifestyle; and individuals with obesity tend to have lower incomes, with women facing greater wage penalties than men. Experts emphasize that responding to the broad social impacts of obesity requires systemic measures, including early prevention, improving food environments, enhancing access to healthy foods, promoting multisectoral collaboration, strengthening primary healthcare and nutrition education, improving professional training, and increasing investment in relevant research.

Health.title: Integration of Mental Health into National Health Strategies
Health.content: The incorporation of mental health services into national health strategies in the United Kingdom is reflected in the systematic integration of psychological therapies into primary care, as explicitly advanced in the NHS Long Term Plan and the NHS Mental Health Implementation Plan (2019/20–2023/24). These national strategies call for a significant expansion of access to psychological therapies, enabling more people with common mental health conditions such as anxiety and depression to receive IAPT (Improving Access to Psychological Therapies) services within primary care settings. They also extend integrated IAPT-LTC services for individuals with long-term physical conditions, ensuring that all patients have access to NICE-recommended psychological interventions while promoting collaboration among mental and physical healthcare providers and the development of jointly coordinated care plans. Analyses from early implementer sites indicate that this integrated model can substantially reduce emergency attendances, hospital admissions, and general practitioner appointments related to diabetes, cardiovascular, and respiratory conditions. Locating mental health therapists within general practice allows patients to receive psychological support at an early stage of their healthcare needs, while also facilitating knowledge sharing among professionals and enhancing the team’s capacity to deliver comprehensive and holistic care. This integration model not only strengthens the primary care system’s service capacity but also advances the delivery of more coordinated, efficient, and accessible mental health services within the national health system.

US-Elections.title: Youth Electoral Participation and Civic Engagement in the 2020 U.S. Election
US-Elections.content: American youth aged 18–29 demonstrated significant electoral participation and sustained civic engagement in 2020, with voter turnout reaching one of the highest levels in decades. Large numbers of young people were actively involved both before and after the election in various forms of political and social action, such as persuading peers to vote, registering new voters, participating in demonstrations, discussing political issues, and advocating for racial justice. Surveys show that most young people believe they have both the responsibility and the ability to bring about change in the country, and they broadly view elections as an important means of influencing social issues—especially violence against people of color. Supporters of different candidates differed in their sense of political efficacy, levels of engagement, and policy priorities, but shared common ground on issues such as job creation, tax reduction, and “uniting Americans.” Overall, youth not only played a critical role in the 2020 election but also demonstrated a strong willingness to remain engaged in democracy and public affairs going forward.

US-Elections.title: Democratic Stability and the January 6 Capitol Breach
US-Elections.content: On January 6, 2021, the U.S. Capitol was violently breached by a mob attempting to disrupt the peaceful transfer of presidential power and undermine the certification of legitimate election results. During the attack, numerous Capitol Police officers defended this critical component of the democratic process, and afterward, a civil rights organization filed a lawsuit on behalf of eight of them, seeking to hold accountable multiple parties—including Donald Trump, “Stop the Steal,” the Proud Boys, the Oath Keepers, and other extremist groups and individuals. The public statement emphasized that the breach was driven by white supremacist rhetoric and deliberate misinformation, posing a direct threat to democratic governance. It also noted that some politicians continue to oppose accountability measures and that several states have advanced restrictive voting laws, allowing forces that undermine democratic stability to persist. The statement called on people across society to reject violence, hatred, and manipulation of the electoral system in order to safeguard the resilience of democracy.

US-Elections.title: Biden–Trump Rematch and Polarization in the 2024 Election
US-Elections.content: In the 2024 U.S. presidential election, a rematch between Biden and Trump has increasingly become a foregone conclusion, yet polls indicate that many Americans are deeply dissatisfied with this choice and broadly hope for new candidates to emerge. According to the survey, Trump leads Biden by six percentage points nationwide, and a significant share of respondents express disillusionment with the two-party system, believing that the current slate of candidates fails to represent broader public expectations. The election exhibits pronounced political polarization: among Biden supporters, more than half are motivated primarily by opposition to Trump, while Trump supporters are similarly driven by negative views of Biden and Democratic policies. Voters harbor substantial concerns about the age and capability of both candidates, with doubts about whether Biden is too old for the job being especially widespread. In addition, some voters are considering third-party options, reflecting distrust toward the two-party dominance. Although polarized attitudes strongly shape voter behavior, the core bases of both parties may remain highly mobilized; at the same time, Trump’s ongoing legal cases—and the divided views among Republicans about whether he should be convicted—add further uncertainty to the election’s trajectory. This much-criticized rematch has stirred notable division, fatigue, and polarization across American society.

US-Elections.title: Abortion as a Mobilizing Issue in the 2024 U.S. Election
US-Elections.content: After Roe v. Wade was overturned in 2022, abortion emerged as one of the most powerful mobilizing issues in the 2024 U.S. presidential election. Kamala Harris and the Democratic Party placed abortion rights at the center of their campaign, emphasizing that Donald Trump and the conservative Supreme Court justices he appointed are responsible for the loss of nationwide abortion protections. Relevant polling shows that abortion ranks among the most important voting motivations for Democratic voters—especially women and younger voters—and is closely connected to issues such as health care and Supreme Court appointments. Although abortion holds a lower priority among Republican voters, past Republican mobilization around Supreme Court appointments helped bring about this policy shift. Since 2022, the enactment of strict abortion restrictions across various states has further heightened the prominence of the issue, making the experiences of those affected an important basis for Democratic efforts to mobilize voters and thereby strengthening the role of abortion rights as a mobilizing force in the 2024 election.

Regional.title: France's 2021 Reform on Age of Sexual Consent
Regional.content: France's 2021 reform established the age of sexual consent at 15, and 18 in cases involving incest. Under this law, any sexual act involving penetration committed by an adult against a minor under 15 is automatically classified as rape, punishable by 20 years of imprisonment; non-penetrative sexual acts constitute sexual assault, carrying a maximum penalty of 10 years of imprisonment and a fine. The law also includes a “Romeo and Juliet” clause: when the age difference between the parties is less than five years and the minor is at least 13, the act is not automatically considered rape or sexual assault, and authorities must instead investigate whether violence, coercion, threat, or surprise was involved. In incest cases, minors are presumed unable to consent until the age of 18. The reform maintains the 2018 extension of the statute of limitations for sexual crimes against minors to 30 years after the victim reaches adulthood, and introduces a “sliding” limitation period and interruption mechanisms to prevent multiple successive offenses from becoming unprosecutable due to time limits. The law also creates a new offense of sextortion and strengthens penalties for sexual exposure, procuring, and the exploitation of child prostitution.

Regional.title: Fukushima Treated Water Release and Risk Governance
Regional.content: The decision by the Japanese government and Tokyo Electric Power Company(TEPCO) to discharge treated water from the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power plant into the ocean has generated widespread controversy. More than one million cubic meters of radioactive wastewater are currently stored in tanks at the Fukushima site, and about 70% of this water requires reprocessing due to the insufficient performance of the ALPS multi-nuclide removal system. The ecological and health impacts of several radionuclides—such as tritium, carbon-14, strontium-90, and iodine-129—have not been fully evaluated. Although the government claims that discharge is a necessary option, available information indicates that additional storage capacity remains on site. The IAEA has endorsed the discharge plan but has not conducted a comprehensive review of ALPS operations, nor fully addressed the issue of highly radioactive fuel debris that continues to contaminate groundwater. The plan has not undergone a full Environmental Impact Assessment and has been criticized as potentially violating obligations under the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) regarding the prevention of transboundary marine pollution. Multiple governments, UN human rights mechanisms, and civil society organizations have criticized the decision for disregarding the concerns of coastal communities, fishers, and Pacific region states. Reports also indicate that Taiwan's fisheries and aquaculture industries could be affected, and its government will need to negotiate monitoring, testing, and compensation arrangements with Japan.

Regional.title: China’s Post-Pandemic Economic Rebound and Structural Transformation
Regional.content: After three years of stringent pandemic control measures, China moved swiftly toward a full reopening at the end of 2022, accompanied by a policy shift from regulatory tightening to growth support, which led to clear signs of economic improvement. Consumer demand, suppressed for years, began to recover, with restaurant spending, box office revenues, parcel volumes, and domestic travel activities during the Spring Festival rising to high levels; the services PMI also recorded a notable improvement. Meanwhile, the recovery of the labor market and household confidence laid the foundation for a rebound in consumption. As economic activity resumed, China’s influence on global trade, international travel, and commodity markets strengthened again, providing a particularly noticeable boost to external demand in Asia. However, the recovery still faces structural challenges, including continued stress in the property sector, the constraints posed by accelerated population aging on potential growth, and uncertainties arising from geopolitical tensions. Looking ahead, China is promoting technological self-reliance, localization of key links in industrial supply chains, energy security, and green transformation, while generating new strategic opportunities in areas such as healthcare, advanced manufacturing, and renewable energy. These developments together form the core picture of China’s post-pandemic economic rebound and structural transformation.

Regional.title: Canada's Cannabis Industry in the Post-Legalization Era
Regional.content: Since the legalization of cannabis in 2018, Canada's cannabis industry has undergone a clear shift from early investment enthusiasm to its current economic difficulties. Although the market expanded rapidly in the initial phase of legalization, the industry now faces issues such as overproduction, weak profitability, concentrated retail locations, and stringent regulation. Most producers struggle to achieve positive cash flow, and many large companies have resorted to layoffs, asset sales, or diversifying into other businesses to survive. Strict regulatory measures—such as standardized packaging, advertising restrictions, and limits on product types and potency—help protect public health but also constrain the competitiveness of legal businesses, allowing the illicit market to remain attractive in terms of price and product variety. In addition, limited access to international markets prevents Canadian producers from expanding revenue through exports. Despite these challenges, legalization is still regarded as a general policy success, enabling consumers across the country to obtain regulated products. The long-term viability and development of the industry, however, depend on finding a more balanced approach between regulation and market demand.

Regional.title: U.S. Clean Energy Transformation
Regional.content: The United States is undergoing a significant clean energy transformation, characterized by record-breaking growth in solar and battery storage installations, the dominance of renewables in new power capacity additions, and the rapid expansion of domestic clean energy manufacturing. Between 2023 and 2024, solar power, battery storage, and electric vehicle sales all reached new highs, and the share of renewables in the U.S. power system continued to rise. States across the country have advanced new clean energy commitments and climate policies, while federal legislation such as the Inflation Reduction Act, the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law, and the CHIPS and Science Act has driven increased investment in the nation's clean energy supply chain and manufacturing sector. However, this transformation faces substantial headwinds, including insufficient transmission capacity, lengthy interconnection queues, local opposition to project siting, high interest rates, persistent supply-chain bottlenecks, and policy uncertainties created by actions of the Trump administration. These challenges are becoming even more pressing as U.S. electricity demand grows, driven in part by data centers, underscoring the urgency of accelerating clean energy development.
